channel.title: Edric Tam on Edric Tam
channel.description: Recent content in Edric Tam on Edric Tam

item.title: A Receptor and Neuron that Activate a Circuit Limiting Sucrose Consumption
Deep Learning
item.description: When I was a sophomore in college, I worked on a project with Dr. Jianqiao Feng (now at Google) and Prof. Michael I Miller at JHU to classify human faces in a sizable image dataset using a Convolutional Neural Net. It was before Tensorflow was a thing, and Facebook at that time has just published their DeepFace paper. We used Theano in Python and achieved classification results that were not far away from the state of the art results at that time. Jianqiao did much of the heavy lifting, and I mainly did the computer vision tasks and feature extraction etc. If you are an employer and would like to take a look at our research report, let me know!

item.title: Handwriting
item.description:
This was a project performed at the Kennedy Krieger Institute at JHU with Ben Dirlikov, Prof. Stewart Mostofsky, Prof. Amy Bastian and one of my faculty advisors Prof. Laurent Younes.

It is known that children with Autism Spectrum Disorder often suffer from dysgraphia. We wanted to develop an automated approach for handwriting assessment in these children, which would facilitate research projects further down. So I wrote an iOS app for iPad to collect handwriting data from these children, where the time and pixel coordinates are uploaded in real time to Parse (Firebase wasn’t a thing back then…and Parse wasn’t acquired by Facebook yet). Subsequent morphometrical analysis using algorithms like LDDMM could then provide scoring/assessment.

The results of this project were presented in a poster at the 2015 International Meeting For Autism Research (IMFAR). You can find the poster here.

item.title: JHU NewsLetter
item.description:
“The Johns Hopkins News-Letter” (sometimes referred to affectionately as “The John Hopkin New Letter”…seriously please don’t do it) is a student newspaper at JHU. It is first published in 1896 and is one of the nation’s oldest college newspapers of its kind.

JHUNL wanted a new website and new design. Then Editors-in-Chief Samhita Ilango and Melanie Levine wanted me to redesign/rewrite the website within a relatively short period of time, so I turned to the good old LAMP stack and Wordpress. Perhaps not the most technically sophisticated project that I have worked on, but the website was up and running in no time.

One pleasant surprise of the project was the legacy left: if you visit https://www.jhunewsletter.com/ today, it is still the same website that I built with essentially the same design. :D

item.title: OcuRex
item.description:
I worked on this project as part of a Johns Hopkins BME Design team when I was a freshman in college.

Our team tried to come up with a novel device to allow for fast and automated fundus imaging in a non-mydriatic manner. The application in mind was to diagnose of glaucoma cheaply and quickly at rural areas in developing countries.

A provisional patent for this work has been filed. You can find the patent here.

P.S. You will see my name on the patent filing as “Eric Tam”…apparently somebody (probably from the JHU Tech Transfer Office) misspelled my name. (Yes, people misspell my name a lot. Yes, I have emailed the tech transfer office about it. No, there is not much that we could do to modify it at this point…)

item.title: PKPD and Cancer
item.description:
This was a project I worked on at Johns Hopkins with my buddies Martin Kang (Mount Sinai), Pat Bovonratwet (Yale), and Kyle Xiong (Carnegie Mellon), as well as Professor Marc Donohue (Johns Hopkins). We developed a PK-PD model for using Chemotherapy against tumors. We then seeked to optimize the dosing regimen under a simple objective function to minimize both the mass of the tumor and any collateral damage. It was a fun research project incorporating various compartment models as well as some optimal control theory.

Now that I’m a PhD student at Duke, I wonder if any Reinforcement Learning or Bayesian approach would work for this project. More to come soon!

item.title: SpeechBanana
item.description:
This is a project that I have worked on with my research advisor and mentor Tilak Ratnanather and a team of students at the Center for Imaging Science at JHU.

The description below is taken from the project’s official website.

Learning to interpret speech provided by a cochlear implant or hearing aid for the first time is an extensive and ongoing process, often taking months to years for people with hearing loss to master complex tasks such as speech comprehension, and partly dependent on how often they can practice using their device. Auditory training (AT) programs, which allow the person with hearing loss to build up the individual listening skills needed to understand spoken communication, are critically beneficial to this learning process. Despite the benefits of AT and the ongoing nature of auditory learning, people with hearing loss in many countries face considerable barriers to accessing regular sessions, including limited coverage by insurance and limited availability of clinical training programs. Computerized training applications thus offer a useful supplement to in-person training, filling in the gaps between less frequent trips to the clinic. >

During my senior year, I led the programming/software development efforts on this project. After my graduation, the team has continued to update and improve the app. A Korean version is now available!

You can find the English version of the iOS app here and the Korean version here

item.title: Axon Dynamics during Neocortical Laminar Innervation
Determining Bruise Etiology in Muscle Tissue Using Finite Element Analysis
